module_category: General Skills: Orientation and Analyzing Behavior
title: Missing-Links Analysis Ask the following questions to understand how and why effective behavior that is needed or expected did not occur.

pages.text:
23
From DBT Skills Training Handouts and Worksheets, Second Edition by Marsha M. Linehan. Copyright 2015 by Marsha M. Linehan. Permission
to photocopy this handout is granted to purchasers of this book for personal use only (see copyright page for details).
General Handout 8 (General Worksheet 3)
Missing-Links Analysis
Ask the following questions to understand how and why
effective behavior that is needed or expected did not occur.
1. Did you know what effective behavior was needed or expected (what skills
homework was given, what skills to use, etc.)?
If no to Question 1, ask what got in the way of knowing what was needed or expected.
Ideas might include not paying attention, unclear instructions, never getting the instructions
in the first place, becoming too overwhelmed and couldn’t process the information, and so
on.
Problem-solve what got in the way. For example, you might work on paying
attention, ask for clarification when you don’t understand instructions, call others, look
up information, and so on.
2. If yes to Question 1, ask were you willing to do the needed or expected effective
behavior?
If no to Question 2, ask what got in the way of willingness to do effective behaviors.
Ideas might include willfulness, feeling inadequate, or feeling demoralized.
Problem-solve what got in the way of willingness. For example, you might practice
radical acceptance, do pros and cons, practice opposite action, and so on.
3. If yes to Question 2, ask did the thought of doing the needed or expected effective
behavior ever enter your mind?
If no to Question 3,
Problem-solve how to get the thought of doing effective behaviors into your mind.
For example, you might put it on your calendar, set your alarm to go off, put your skills
notebook next to your bed, practice coping ahead with difficult situations (see Emotion
Regulation Handout 19), and so on.
4. If yes to Question 3, ask what got in the way of doing the needed or expected
effective behavior right away? Ideas might include putting it off, continuing to
procrastinate, not being in the mood, forgetting how to do what was needed, thinking that
no one would care anyway (or no one would find out), and so on.
Problem-solve what got in the way. For example, you might set a reward for doing
what is expected, practice opposite action, do pros and cons, and so on.

General Handout 8 (General Worksheet 3)
Missing-Links Analysis
Ask the following questions to understand how and why
effective behavior that is needed or expected did not occur.
1. Did you know what effective behavior was needed or expected (what skills
homework was given, what skills to use, etc.)?
If no to Question 1, ask what got in the way of knowing what was needed or expected.
Ideas might include not paying attention, unclear instructions, never getting the instructions
in the first place, becoming too overwhelmed and couldn’t process the information, and so
on.
Problem-solve what got in the way. For example, you might work on paying
attention, ask for clarification when you don’t understand instructions, call others, look
up information, and so on.
2. If yes to Question 1, ask were you willing to do the needed or expected effective
behavior?
If no to Question 2, ask what got in the way of willingness to do effective behaviors.
Ideas might include willfulness, feeling inadequate, or feeling demoralized.
Problem-solve what got in the way of willingness. For example, you might practice
radical acceptance, do pros and cons, practice opposite action, and so on.
3. If yes to Question 2, ask did the thought of doing the needed or expected effective
behavior ever enter your mind?
If no to Question 3,
Problem-solve how to get the thought of doing effective behaviors into your mind.
For example, you might put it on your calendar, set your alarm to go off, put your skills
notebook next to your bed, practice coping ahead with difficult situations (see Emotion
Regulation Handout 19), and so on.
4. If yes to Question 3, ask what got in the way of doing the needed or expected
effective behavior right away? Ideas might include putting it off, continuing to
procrastinate, not being in the mood, forgetting how to do what was needed, thinking that

Problem-solve what got in the way. For example, you might set a reward for doing
what is expected, practice opposite action, do pros and cons, and so on.

ourspace_kindness_pass.title: Missing-Links Analysis Ask the following questions to understand how and why effective behavior that is needed or expected did not occur.
ourspace_kindness_pass.category: DBT General Orientation and Analyzing Behavior

main_doability_needs: bed-level or seated options by default
oxygen/breathing, pain, vertigo, fatigue, mobility, and recovery time respected
ADHD/autism supports: transitions, low-stimulation starts, tiny visible steps
partial completion and stopping before a crash count as success
no shame, no body blame, no pressure to push through pain or dysphoria
ADHD transition support before task demands
caregiver burnout recovery treated as care, not avoidance
protected rest, boundaries, decompression, sensory needs, and people-pleasing checks
no assumption that Jasper has unlimited caregiver capacity

household_rules: no hour block may hold more than two tasks/tools
skills may be used as one-breath or one-field versions
survival days load critical care first and skip optional worksheets
rest, boundaries, decompression, and asking for help count as care
skills are for support, not grades

universal_kindness_rules: This is a support tool, not a test.
One line, one checkbox, one breath, or one tiny step counts.
Skipping a skill because the body is unsafe, exhausted, dysregulated, or overloaded is allowed.
Do not stack this with more than one other task inside the same hour.
Survival-day version always wins over perfection.
william_accessibility_adaptations: Use bed-level, seated, voice-note, or caregiver-assisted versions when needed.
Avoid pain escalation, respiratory strain, unsafe transfers, and dysphoria-triggering language.
Break worksheets into one-field chunks; stopping before a crash is success.
jasper_accessibility_adaptations: Start with transition support before expecting focus.
Protect caregiver burnout recovery; rest and boundaries are valid completion.
Use body-doubling, timers, low-stimulation versions, and one-minute starts.
tiny_steps: Choose one sentence or tool that fits today.
Use the low-spoon version; no full worksheet is required.

routine_integration.start_the_day_use: eligible as a rotating daily skill, never more than one DBT/ADHD skill in an hour block
routine_integration.recommended_block: after body check or before decompression
